subject: [PATCH] mm: vmscan: abort proactive reclaim early when freezing

reviews.author: Richard Chang
reviews.summary: Clarified that global/memcg reclaim has a small target batch size and checks for signals between attempts, whereas proactive reclaim can have an extremely large batch size without signal checks, leading to potential freezing issues.
reviews.raw_body:
On Wed, Jul 8, 2026 at 12:54 PM Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev> wrote:
>
> What's so special about proactive reclaim? Why the same situation can not happen
> for global or memcg reclaim?
>

The target batch size of the global/memcg reclaim is typically very
small -- usually SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX (32 pages), allowing the task to
yield or check signals between attempts.
Furthermore, kswapd is a kernel thread designed to be freezable, it
explicitly checks kthread_freezable_should_stop() in the main loop.
In contrast, the target batch size in proactive reclaim is
user-defined and can be extremely large. Without signal checks in the
inner loops, the task may continue scanning and evicting pages for
seconds at a time.

reviews.author: T.J. Mercier
reviews.summary: Pointed out that proactive reclaim is slow and suggested fixing the underlying issue instead of just aborting it early, but also acknowledged that this is orthogonal to the current patch.
reviews.raw_body:
On Wed, Jul 8, 2026 at 8:48 AM Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev> wrote:
>
> On Wed, Jul 08, 2026 at 03:24:15PM +0800, Richard Chang wrote:
> > On Wed, Jul 8, 2026 at 12:54 PM Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev> wrote:
> > >
> > > What's so special about proactive reclaim? Why the same situation can not happen
> > > for global or memcg reclaim?
> > >
> >
> > The target batch size of the global/memcg reclaim is typically very
> > small -- usually SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX (32 pages), allowing the task to
> > yield or check signals between attempts.
> > Furthermore, kswapd is a kernel thread designed to be freezable, it
> > explicitly checks kthread_freezable_should_stop() in the main loop.
> > In contrast, the target batch size in proactive reclaim is
> > user-defined and can be extremely large. Without signal checks in the
> > inner loops, the task may continue scanning and evicting pages for
> > seconds at a time.
>
> Why can't the proactive reclaim use the same batch size as memcg and global
> reclaim?

Because it is very slow:
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20240202233855.1236422-1-tjmercier@google.com/

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt
reviews.summary: Questioned why the patch doesn't address the underlying issue of slow proactive reclaim and suggested that a single solution for all types of reclaim would be better, but also clarified that this is out of scope for the current patch.
reviews.raw_body:
On Wed, Jul 08, 2026 at 03:24:15PM +0800, Richard Chang wrote:
> On Wed, Jul 8, 2026 at 12:54\u202fPM Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev> wrote:
> >
> > What's so special about proactive reclaim? Why the same situation can not happen
> > for global or memcg reclaim?
> >
> 
> The target batch size of the global/memcg reclaim is typically very
> small -- usually SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX (32 pages), allowing the task to
> yield or check signals between attempts.
> Furthermore, kswapd is a kernel thread designed to be freezable, it
> explicitly checks kthread_freezable_should_stop() in the main loop.
> In contrast, the target batch size in proactive reclaim is
> user-defined and can be extremely large. Without signal checks in the
> inner loops, the task may continue scanning and evicting pages for
> seconds at a time.

Why can't the proactive reclaim use the same batch size as memcg and global
reclaim?

2026-07-08.patch_summary: Patch proposes to abort proactive reclaim early when freezing, but reviewer questions why the same situation can't happen for global or memcg reclaim.
